nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral zone of Lake Tanganyika; inhabits crevices, caves, and the spaces between piled boulders along the lake's steep rocky coastline

decorPreferences: piled rock formations with multiple caves and crevices
PVC tubes or ceramic caves as spawning sites
rocky substrate (crushed coral, aragonite, or Lake Tanganyika sand)
minimal planting — tough plants such as Vallisneria or Anubias tolerated
clear open areas between rock structures

diet.notes: In the wild, the Leleupi feeds primarily on small invertebrates, insect larvae, and zooplankton gleaned from rock surfaces. In the aquarium, it generally accepts high-quality carnivore-formula pellets or micro-pellets, frozen or freeze-dried bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, mysis shrimp, and Cyclops. Live or frozen invertebrate foods are particularly useful for conditioning spawning pairs. Plant-based or high-carbohydrate foods are best avoided, as a diet too rich in plant matter can cause digestive issues.

goodWith: other rock-dwelling Tanganyikan cichlids of similar size (Julidochromis, Altolamprologus)
shell-dwelling Lamprologines (Neolamprologus multifasciatus, N. brevis) in larger tanks
open-water Tanganyikan schoolers (Cyprichromis, Paracyprichromis) that occupy mid-water
Synodontis catfish native to Lake Tanganyika
avoidWith: small or docile fish that will be harassed or eaten
other Neolamprologus leleupi unless in a very large tank with ample territory
aggressive mbuna or other species that will outcompete them at rock structures
tetras and other unrelated small community fish

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Neolamprologus leleupi; depicts typical brilliant yellow-orange coloration of the Kapampa locality form.
Wikimedia Commons image; clear lateral view of adult Neolamprologus leleupi showing characteristic yellow-orange body coloration.

summary: The Leleupi, also called the Lemon Cichlid (Neolamprologus leleupi), is a brilliantly yellow-to-orange rock-dwelling cichlid from Lake Tanganyika prized for its vivid coloration. It forms monogamous pairs, breeds readily in caves, and requires the hard, alkaline water chemistry typical of the Rift Lakes.
careNotes:
The Leleupi is among the most visually striking cichlids from Lake Tanganyika, displaying a brilliant yellow-to-deep-orange body that varies noticeably by collection locality. The Kapampa form (southern DRC) tends toward deep orange, the Bemba form (northwestern Tanzania) toward lemon yellow, and the Karilani form is paler. These locality variants are sometimes available from specialist breeders and are generally advised not to be hybridised.

Like all Lake Tanganyika cichlids, the Leleupi requires hard, alkaline, and highly stable water chemistry — pH 8.0–9.0, gH 10–20, kH 8–18, temperature 24–27°C. A quality pH buffer or the use of coral sand or crushed aragonite substrate is advisable to maintain the high pH. Weekly water changes of 20–30% support nitrate control without destabilising the chemistry. Ammonia and nitrite must be zero; Tanganyika cichlids are considered particularly sensitive to water quality lapses.

The Leleupi is strictly rock-dwelling and must be provided with a well-structured aquascape featuring multiple caves, overhangs, and crevices built from smooth or lava rock. Each pair will claim a territory centred on a cave and tends to defend it vigorously against conspecifics and similarly sized intruders. A minimum 120 L tank with a 90 cm footprint is recommended for a single pair, with tankmates ideally occupying different niches. In a Tanganyika community, mid-water schooling species such as Cyprichromis work well because they do not compete for rocky territories.

Feeding should centre on protein-rich carnivore foods — high-quality pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, and mysis. The Leleupi is an active hunter and will pick invertebrates from rockwork. Multiple small feedings daily are preferable to one large meal. Dwarf shrimp and other small invertebrates are likely to be eaten and are best excluded from the same aquarium.
breedingNotes:
Neolamprologus leleupi is a substrate-spawning cave breeder that forms lasting monogamous pair bonds. Spawning is triggered by stable water quality, good nutrition, and the presence of a suitable enclosed spawning site — a cave, overturned pot, ceramic tube, or rocky crevice. The female deposits 50–200 adhesive eggs on the cave ceiling or wall; both parents guard the clutch and the resulting fry vigorously.

Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 25–26°C; fry become free-swimming after a further 5–7 days and remain under tight parental supervision for several weeks. Free-swimming fry are large enough to accept freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and finely crushed micro-pellets from the outset. Parents are highly protective and territorial while breeding — conspecifics and even previously tolerated tankmates may be attacked; a species-only or very spacious tank is advisable during spawning. Remove grown juveniles once they are large enough to risk being harmed by renewed parental aggression or subsequent spawning activity. Conditioning pairs with live or frozen invertebrate foods significantly improves spawn frequency and brood size.

Used as authoritative basis for water parameters and husbandry.

fish.breedingNotes: The Leleupi is a cave-spawning species that forms a lasting monogamous pair bond. The female deposits 50–200 adhesive eggs on the ceiling or wall of a cave or enclosed surface, and both parents guard the clutch together. Eggs typically hatch in approximately 48–72 hours, with fry becoming free-swimming after a further 5–7 days. Free-swimming fry are large enough from the outset to accept freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and finely crushed micro-pellets. Conditioning the pair with live or frozen invertebrate foods is generally advisable to encourage spawning. Parents tend to become highly aggressive toward tankmates during the breeding period, and a species-only setup or a spacious tank is often recommended at this stage.
